You said:
I am trying to import to ESCO dataset.I want to use their ontology, instead of building my own ontology using the Oasis dataset I found a github code already treating the data in csv, but I want to transform all of it into a graph structure. I want to build a clean data pipineline, that will ingest the data, transform it in the required format, perform the link between the tables, format it as a graph and then plot it into a graph visualisation I could also have each of the table in SQL if desire. Although, now I am debating if I should clone the repo, in my current Navigo repo? Is it gonna have to much content in my repo? Skills File Description File: skills.csv Number of Skills: 13897 Number of Columns: 10 Skill Groups File Description File: skill_groups.csv Number of Skill Groups: 641 Number of Columns: 8 Occupations File Description File: occupations.csv Number of Occupations: 3007 Number of Columns: 13 ISCO Groups File Description File: isco_groups.csv Number of ISCO Groups: 619 Number of Columns: 7 Skill-Skill Relations File Description File: skill_to_skill_relations.csv Number of Relations: 6899 Number of Columns: 3 Occupation-to-Skill Relations File Description File: occupation_to_skill_relations.csv Number of Relations: 123789 Number of Columns: 4 Skill Hierarchy File Description File Name: skill_hierarchy.csv Number of Relations: 20650 Number of Columns: 4 Occupation Hierarchy File Description File Name: occupation_hierarchy.csv Number of Relations: 3617 Number of Columns: 4 Tabiya CSV Documentation The tabiya CSV format is used to import and export data from the Tabiya Open Taxonomy platform. There are 9 CSV files in the format. Each file contains a different type of data. The files are: Model Info Skill Groups Skills Skill Hierarchy Skill to Skill Relations ISCO Groups Occupations Occupation Hierarchy Occupation to Skill Relations General notes on the fields of the CSV files UUID History A UUIDHISTORY field is a list of all the UUIDs that have been assigned to an entity during its lifecycle, e.g. when the entity is created, imported, exported or copied into our platform. It is an identifier that can be used for tracking objects not only across their lifecycle, but also across systems. The UUID history is ordered from newest to oldest UUID. The first entry in the list is the current UUID of the object. The last entry in the list is the very first (initial) UUID of the object. The entities in this dataset have been assigned an initial UUID. When an entity is imported into our platform, a new UUID will be issued and added at the top of UUID history. The UUID used by the platform are based on the Universally Unique Identifier v4 standard. The maximum number of UUIDs in the history for an object is constrained to 10000. Origin Uri The ORIGINURI field is a URI that points to the location where an entity was originally defined. The maximum length for the Origin Uri is 4096 characters. ID The ID field is a unique identifier for each entity in the CSV dataset. It is used for referencing within the CSV dataset, for example, in the relations between entities. This field is not meant to be used as an identifier outside the scope of the CSV files, for that purpose you should use the first entry in the UUID History. Object Types The object types are used to differentiate between different types of entities in the dataset. For example in relations between entities, the object types are used to specify the type of the parent and child objects and determine in which file these objects can be located. The object types in the CSV files are: skill: Represents a skill. skillgroup: Represents a skill group. escooccupation: Represents an occupation that originates from the ESCO framework. localoccupation: Represents an occupation that not originate from the ESCO framework and is defined only this taxonomy. iscogroup: Represents an ISCO group. Lists List properties are stored in the CSV files as strings separated by a \n character. Currently, we do not support values that contain a new line. Dates The dates in the CSV files are stored in the ISO 8601 format. File descriptions Model Info Contains information about the model. The export filename is model_info.csv Columns UUIDHISTORY: A list of UUIDs. NAME: The name of the model. LOCALE: The short code of the model's locale. DESCRIPTION: The description of the model. VERSION: The version of the model. RELEASED: A boolean value that indicates whether the model is released or not. RELEASENOTES: The release notes of the model. CREATEDAT: The date the model was created. UPDATEDAT: The date the model was last updated. Skills Contains the skills of the taxonomy. The export filename is skills.csv Columns ORIGINURI: A URI that points to the location where the skill was originally defined. ID: A unique identifier, used for referencing the skill within the CSV dataset. UUIDHISTORY: A list of UUIDs. SKILLTYPE: The skill type. Possible values: skill/competence,knowledge,language,attitude or empty ( ). REUSELEVEL: The skill reuse level. Possible values: sector-specific,occupation-specific,cross-sector,transversal or empty ( ). PREFERREDLABEL: The preferred label of the skill. ALTLABELS: A list of alternative labels for the skill. Maximum length per label: 256 characters. Maximum number of labels: 100. DESCRIPTION: The skill description. Maximum length:4000 characters. DEFINITION: The skill definition. Maximum length:4000 characters. SCOPENOTE: The skill scope note. Maximum length:4000 characters. CREATEDAT: The date the skill was created. UPDATEDAT: The date the skill was last updated. Skill Groups Contains the skill groups of the taxonomy. The export filename is skill_groups.csv Columns ORIGINURI: A URI that points to the location where the skill group was originally defined. ID: A unique identifier, used for referencing the skill group within the CSV dataset. UUIDHISTORY: A list of UUIDs. CODE: SkillGroup code as defined in ESCO. It has the general format SX.X.X, where X is a number. PREFERREDLABEL: The preferred label of the skill group. ALTLABELS: A list of alternative labels for the skill group. Maximum length per label: 256 characters. Maximum number of labels: 100. DESCRIPTION: The skill group description. Maximum length:4000 characters. SCOPENOTE: The skill group scope note. Maximum length:4000 characters. CREATEDAT: The date the skill group was created. UPDATEDAT: The date the skill group was last updated. Occupations Contains the occupations of the taxonomy. The export filename is occupations.csv Columns ORIGINURI: A URI that points to the location where the occupation was originally defined. ID: A unique identifier, used for referencing the occupation within the CSV dataset. UUIDHISTORY: A list of UUIDs. ISCOGROUPCODE: A four digit identification code of the ISCO group that the occupation belongs to. CODE: An occupation code assigned to the occupation. PREFERREDLABEL: The preferred label of the occupation. ALTLABELS: A list of alternative labels for the occupation. Maximum length per label: 256 characters. Maximum number of labels: 100. DESCRIPTION: The occupation description. Maximum length:4000 characters. DEFINITION: The occupation definition. Maximum length:4000 characters. SCOPENOTE: The occupation scope note. Maximum length:4000 characters. REGULATEDPROFESSIONNOTE: The regulated profession note. Maximum length:4000 characters. OCCUPATIONTYPE: The type of the occupation. Possible values: escooccupation or localoccupation. ISLOCALIZED: A boolean value that indicates whether the occupation is localized or not. Only ocuppations of the type escooccupation can be localized. Possible values: true or false. CREATEDAT: The date the occupation was created. UPDATEDAT: The date the occupation was last updated. ISCO Groups Contains the ISCO groups of the taxonomy. The export filename is isco_groups.csv Columns ORIGINURI: A URI that points to the location where the ISCO group was originally defined. ID: A unique identifier, used for referencing the ISCO group within the CSV dataset. UUIDHISTORY: A list of UUIDs. CODE: A four digit identification code of the ISCO group. PREFERREDLABEL: The preferred label of the ISCO group. ALTLABELS: A list of alternative labels for the ISCO group. Maximum length per label: 256 characters. Maximum number of labels: 100. DESCRIPTION: The ISCO group description. Maximum length:4000 characters. CREATEDAT: The date the ISCO group was created. UPDATEDAT: The date the ISCO group was last updated. Skill-to-Skill Relations Contains the relations between skills. The export filename is skill_to_skill_relations.csv Columns REQUIRINGID: The ID of the skill that requires another skill. RELATIONTYPE: The type of the relation. Possible values: essential or optional. REQUIREDID: The ID of the skill that is required by another skill. CREATEDAT: The date the relation was created. UPDATEDAT: The date the relation was last updated. Occupation-to-Skill Relations Contains the relations between occupations and skills. The export filename is occupation_to_skill_relations.csv Columns OCCUPATIONTYPE: The type of the occupation. Possible values: escooccupation or localoccupation. OCCUPATIONID: The ID of the occupation. RELATIONTYPE: The type of the relation. Possible values: essential or optional. SKILLID: The ID of the skill. CREATEDAT: The date the relation was created. UPDATEDAT: The date the relation was last updated. Skill Hierarchy Contains the hierarchical structure of various skills. The export filename is skill_hierarchy.csv Columns PARENTOBJECTTYPE: The type of the parent object. Possible values: skill or skillgroup. PARENTID: The ID of the parent object. CHILDID: The ID of the child object. CHILDOBJECTTYPE: The type of the child object. Possible values: skill or skillgroup. CREATEDAT: The date the relation was created. UPDATEDAT: The date the relation was last updated. Caveat: A skill cannot be the parent of a skill group. Occupation Hierarchy Contains the hierarchical structure of various occupations. The export filename is occupation_hierarchy.csv Columns PARENTOBJECTTYPE: The type of the parent object. Possible values: iscogroup, escooccupation, localoccupation. PARENTID: The ID of the parent object. CHILDID: The ID of the child object. CHILDOBJECTTYPE: The type of the child object. Possible values: iscogroup, escooccupation, localoccupation. CREATEDAT: The date the relation was created. UPDATEDAT: The date the relation was last updated. Caveat: An escooccupation cannot be the parent of an 'iscogroup'. Caveat: An localoccupation can be a child of an escooccupation or another localoccupation.
ChatGPT said:

You’re on a very promising path by choosing to ingest the ESCO dataset and convert it into a graph-based ontology instead of building one from scratch. This move can supercharge your career recommendation platform by aligning with a standardized, Europe-wide taxonomy—great for extensibility, semantic richness, and international scalability.

Here’s a critically reasoned plan based on your current context (Navigo app, FastAPI backend, PostgreSQL on Railway, and graph goals):

✅ Step 1: Should You Clone the Repo into Navigo?

Short answer: No, not directly into the main repo.

Why not:

  • The ESCO parser repo is likely heavy, script-oriented, and tailored for data prep—not production-grade integration.

  • Mixing it with Navigo introduces technical debt, bloats your repo, and breaks separation of concerns.

Instead:

  • Clone it into a separate esco-ingestion subrepo or data layer module.

  • Write ETL scripts that export cleaned tables into your PostgreSQL (already hosted on Railway).

  • Create one-time or scheduled ingestion jobs.

✅ Step 2: Graph Schema Design (from ESCO CSVs)

You have a rich, clean CSV schema—perfect for a knowledge graph. Here’s a conceptual schema:

Nodes (Vertices)

Node TypeSource FileProperties
Skillskills.csvlabel, type, reuse_level
Skill Groupskill_groups.csvlabel, code
Occupationoccupations.csvlabel, ISCO code
ISCO Groupisco_groups.csvlabel, code

Edges (Relationships)

Relationship TypeSource FileDirection & Labels
REQUIRESskill_to_skill_relations.csv(Skill)-[:REQUIRES]->(Skill)
HAS_SKILLoccupation_to_skill_relations.csv(Occupation)-[:HAS_SKILL {type: essential/optional}]->(Skill)
PART_OFskill_hierarchy.csv(Skill/Group)-[:PART_OF]->(Group)
BELONGS_TOoccupation_hierarchy.csv(Occupation/Group)-[:BELONGS_TO]->(Group/Occupation)

✅ Step 3: Clean Data Pipeline

Tech Stack Suggestion:

  • Ingestion scripts: Python with pandas, networkx, and SQLAlchemy.

  • Graph Format:

    • Load as tables in PostgreSQL (intermediate step)

    • Convert to networkx or igraph for runtime processing and visual layout

    • Use pyvis, Plotly, or Graphistry for front-end rendering.

Pipeline Steps:

  1. Raw Data Ingestion: CSV → Pandas

  2. Normalization: Clean fields, drop unused columns, handle lists (\n separated fields → list objects)

  3. Relational Linking: Build join keys (e.g., occupation_id, skill_id)

  4. Graph Construction:

    • Convert to edge list format

    • Build undirected/directed graph in networkx

  5. Optional: Dump to PostgreSQL graph-style tables (Nodes, Edges) or Neo4j if you move that direction.

✅ Step 4: Integration in Navigo

Use the backend layer (FastAPI) to:

  • Expose REST API or GraphQL for retrieving skills, occupations, paths.

  • Trigger the similarity search or recommendation logic using precomputed graph embeddings or traversal logic (e.g., BFS for related roles, shortest paths for upskilling).

✅ Step 5: Visual Graph Exploration in Frontend

Use:

  • react-force-graph or vis-network via a new tab /explore-graph

  • Serve JSON nodes/edges from your API

  • Let students explore “paths between occupations”, “what skills connect two jobs”, “if I master this skill, which roles open up?”

🔁 Strategic Consideration

Why use ESCO?

  • Standardized labels + multilingual + hierarchical

  • Easier to explain to educational partners

  • Extensible to international markets

Why still keep your OaSIS vectors and interests?

  • For personalized matching and narrative-style exploration

  • Blend "official" ontology with personalized discovery paths (like C3-IoC does).

Would you like a clean, minimal code snippet to bootstrap the graph building from these CSVs into networkx or SQL?

You said:
should I train a GNN on the graph?
ChatGPT said:

Training a Graph Neural Network (GNN) on the ESCO-based knowledge graph could be powerful, but only if your goal and data justify it. Let's break it down clearly and critically.

✅ When Should You Train a GNN?

You should consider training a GNN if your use case involves one of the following:

1. Link Prediction (e.g., "What skill is missing from this occupation?")

  • Predict missing skill-to-occupation links.

  • Ideal for recommendation systems or automated upskilling paths.

  • E.g., for a partially known student profile, predict unseen but relevant skills.

2. Node Classification (e.g., classify a skill as 'emerging', 'core', 'obsolete')

  • Useful if you enrich the graph with external labels or market dynamics.

3. Representation Learning for Matching or Search

  • Embed skills and occupations into vector space via GNNs (e.g., using GraphSAGE or GAT).

  • Then use cosine similarity for:

    • Career recommendations

    • "If you liked X, you might like Y"-style suggestions

❌ When GNN is Overkill

You shouldn’t train a GNN yet if:

1. You don’t have labels or supervision

  • No ground truth for tasks like classification, regression, or ranking → no objective.

  • Most ESCO graphs are purely structural (unsupervised), so you’d need downstream labels (e.g., skill importance, skill gaps, salary prediction, etc.).

2. You just want simple exploration or hierarchy traversal

  • Use graph search algorithms (BFS, DFS), shortest paths, and clustering with networkx.

  • These are fast, explainable, and better for building interfaces.

3. You're still building the pipeline

  • A GNN adds infrastructure cost (PyG, DGL, GPU if large).

  • Better to build a clean ETL and baseline graph logic first.

🔄 What’s a Good Middle Ground?

Try node2vec or DeepWalk on the ESCO graph:

  • Unsupervised graph embedding

  • Captures proximity + community

  • Easily usable in your recommendation engine

Then later, if you integrate usage data (e.g., users endorsing skills, students choosing occupations), you can fine-tune a GNN for supervised tasks.

Strategic GNN Use Case for You

Imagine this setup:

  1. Input graph: ESCO (skills, occupations, ISCO groups)

  2. Overlay data: User profiles (skills selected, occupation clicked, courses followed)

  3. Train GNN: On user–skill–occupation subgraph to learn embeddings

  4. Use embeddings:

    • Personalized matching

    • Recommendations

    • Visualization (project into 2D space for map-like experience)

That would give you both structure and personalization — exactly what C3-IoC was trying to build, but with better learning capacity.

Final Recommendation

Don't train a GNN now. Build the graph, explore it, test a simple recommender using node similarity. Once you add user behavior or get downstream labels, move to GNN.

Would you like a walk-through of running node2vec or a GNN starter pipeline in PyTorch Geometric?

You said:
But if I don't train a GNN, then how can the profile of my students be automatically recommended ?
ChatGPT said:

Great question — and here’s the critical distinction:

👉 You don’t need a GNN to do automatic student-to-career matching — at least not initially.

Let’s break down what you do need, and then when a GNN truly adds value.

✅ Option 1: Baseline Recommendation Without GNN

You can build a very effective, explainable hybrid recommender with the ESCO graph + embeddings + student profiles using:

1. Graph Traversal + Filtering

Use the ESCO relations:

  • (Occupation)-[:HAS_SKILL]->(Skill)

  • (Skill)-[:REQUIRES]->(Skill)

  • (Skill)-[:PART_OF]->(SkillGroup)

To:

  • Find careers matching the user’s skills.

  • Recommend occupations where the skill match is ≥ threshold (e.g. 70% essential skills matched).

  • Rank by degree centrality, PageRank, or coverage of key skills.

2. Unsupervised Embeddings

Use node2vec or DeepWalk on the ESCO graph:

  • This gives you latent embeddings of each skill and occupation.

  • You then embed a user profile as:

    python
    user_vector = mean([embedding(skill_id) for skill_id in user.skills])

  • Recommend top-N occupations by cosine similarity between user vector and occupation vectors.

🎯 This works without training a GNN, and it’s interpretable, fast, and reproducible.

✅ Option 2: GNN Comes In When...

Once you have training data — e.g., a log of students clicking, applying, or endorsing jobs/skills — you can train a GNN to learn representations that generalize beyond skill overlap.

For example:

  • You train a GraphSAGE model with:

    • Nodes: skills, occupations, users

    • Edges: HAS_SKILL, SELECTED_OCCUPATION, etc.

    • Task: predict next skill or job selection

GNNs shine when:

  • You have multi-hop dependencies (e.g., skill A is useful not because it's listed in the job, but because it leads to another skill).

  • You want generalization across sparse or noisy profiles.

  • You want joint embedding of structured graph + side information (e.g., text, tags, ratings).

🧠 Strategic Takeaway

Start with unsupervised graph-based methods (like node2vec), build the recommendation pipeline, and validate it. Train a GNN only when you’ve logged enough user behavior to justify it.

This aligns with your goal of having an AI-powered career explorer that guides users — without requiring complex ML from day one.

Would you like a code scaffold for:

  1. node2vec embedding of ESCO graph?

  2. Student profile to occupation similarity ranking?

Or would you prefer we sketch the architecture options side-by-side?

ChatGPT said:

Excellent — you're ready to build a real, production-grade graph pipeline using the ESCO data. Since the data is in structured CSV form and well-documented, we can proceed methodically. Here's a professional-grade plan that breaks this down into 5 pipeline stages with modularity and maintainability in mind:

✅ Pipeline Overview

Input: Your ESCO CSV files
Output: An interactive graph-based visualization of the ESCO skill–occupation–group taxonomy
Stack: Python + Pandas + NetworkX or PyVis (for local), or D3.js frontend (later)

🧱 Directory & File Setup (Assuming this structure)

bash
📁 Orientor_project/
└── 📁 Esco-Oasis/
    └── 📁 taxonomy-model-application/
        └── 📁 data-sets/csv/esco-v1.1.2/
            ├── occupations.csv
            ├── skills.csv
            ├── skill_groups.csv
            ├── occupation_to_skill_relations.csv
            ├── skill_to_skill_relations.csv
            ├── skill_hierarchy.csv
            ├── occupation_hierarchy.csv

⚙️ Step 1: Ingest and Normalize the Data

Create a load_esco_data.py module that:

python
import pandas as pd
from pathlib import Path

DATA_DIR = Path("/Users/philippebeliveau/Desktop/Notebook/Orientor_project/Esco-Oasis/taxonomy-model-application/data-sets/csv/esco-v1.1.2")

def load_esco():
    return {
        "occupations": pd.read_csv(DATA_DIR / "occupations.csv"),
        "skills": pd.read_csv(DATA_DIR / "skills.csv"),
        "skill_groups": pd.read_csv(DATA_DIR / "skill_groups.csv"),
        "occupation_to_skill_relations": pd.read_csv(DATA_DIR / "occupation_to_skill_relations.csv"),
        "skill_to_skill_relations": pd.read_csv(DATA_DIR / "skill_to_skill_relations.csv"),
        "skill_hierarchy": pd.read_csv(DATA_DIR / "skill_hierarchy.csv"),
        "occupation_hierarchy": pd.read_csv(DATA_DIR / "occupation_hierarchy.csv"),
    }

🧠 Step 2: Build the Graph (Directed Multigraph)

Use networkx to build the full taxonomy:

python
import networkx as nx

def build_graph(esco_data):
    G = nx.DiGraph()

    # Add nodes
    for _, row in esco_data['skills'].iterrows():
        G.add_node(f"skill::{row['ID']}", label=row['PREFERREDLABEL'], type='skill')
    
    for _, row in esco_data['skill_groups'].iterrows():
        G.add_node(f"skillgroup::{row['ID']}", label=row['PREFERREDLABEL'], type='skillgroup')

    for _, row in esco_data['occupations'].iterrows():
        G.add_node(f"occupation::{row['ID']}", label=row['PREFERREDLABEL'], type='occupation')

    # Add edges: occupation → skill
    for _, row in esco_data['occupation_to_skill_relations'].iterrows():
        G.add_edge(
            f"occupation::{row['OCCUPATIONID']}",
            f"skill::{row['SKILLID']}",
            type=row['RELATIONTYPE'].lower()  # essential/optional
        )

    # Add edges: skill → skill
    for _, row in esco_data['skill_to_skill_relations'].iterrows():
        G.add_edge(
            f"skill::{row['REQUIRINGID']}",
            f"skill::{row['REQUIREDID']}",
            type=row['RELATIONTYPE'].lower()
        )

    # Add skill hierarchy
    for _, row in esco_data['skill_hierarchy'].iterrows():
        G.add_edge(
            f"{row['PARENTOBJECTTYPE']}::{row['PARENTID']}",
            f"{row['CHILDOBJECTTYPE']}::{row['CHILDID']}",
            type='is_parent_of'
        )

    # Add occupation hierarchy
    for _, row in esco_data['occupation_hierarchy'].iterrows():
        G.add_edge(
            f"{row['PARENTOBJECTTYPE']}::{row['PARENTID']}",
            f"{row['CHILDOBJECTTYPE']}::{row['CHILDID']}",
            type='is_parent_of'
        )

    return G

🌐 Step 3: Export for Visualization

You can:

A. Use pyvis for quick browser-based interactive rendering:

python
from pyvis.network import Network

def visualize_graph(G, output_html="esco_graph.html"):
    net = Network(notebook=False, directed=True)
    for node, data in G.nodes(data=True):
        net.add_node(node, label=data['label'], title=data['type'], color="skyblue" if data['type'] == 'skill' else "lightgreen")

    for src, dst, data in G.edges(data=True):
        net.add_edge(src, dst, label=data['type'])

    net.show(output_html)

B. Or export to JSON for D3.js frontend:

python
import json

def export_to_json(G, path="graph.json"):
    data = {
        "nodes": [{"id": n, "label": d['label'], "type": d['type']} for n, d in G.nodes(data=True)],
        "edges": [{"source": u, "target": v, "type": d['type']} for u, v, d in G.edges(data=True)]
    }
    with open(path, "w") as f:
        json.dump(data, f, indent=2)

🚀 Final Touch: Wrap as CLI Script or Notebook

Wrap all of this in a single run_pipeline.py script or a Jupyter Notebook with sections like:

  • Load CSVs

  • Build Graph

  • Export / Visualize

🔄 Next Steps After This

  • Add support for student profiles (as vector inputs or subgraphs).

  • Compute shortest paths from current skills to target occupation.

  • Overlay recommendation logic using node2vec or skill gap analysis.

  • Serve via FastAPI + stream the graph to frontend via JSON or WebSocket.

ChatGPT said:

Great question — here’s a practical answer based on your development phase and iteration style:

✅ If You're Still Experimenting and Iterating...

Use a Jupyter Notebook.

Why:

  • You can inspect intermediate outputs (df.head(), G.nodes(data=True), etc.).

  • Ideal for testing logic (e.g., weird relations in skill_hierarchy.csv).

  • Easy to visualize with pyvis.show() and tweak layout dynamically.

Suggested structure:

sql
📁 Orientor_project/
└── 📁 notebooks/
    └── build_esco_graph.ipynb   ← (Start here)

✅ If You’re Moving Toward Integration or Reuse...

Modularize into Python files.

Why:

  • You can reuse functions (e.g., load_esco() or build_graph() in your FastAPI backend).

  • Better for testing and productionizing (especially if you plan to schedule updates or serve JSON to frontend).

Suggested module layout:

bash
📁 Orientor_project/
└── 📁 esco_graph/
    ├── __init__.py
    ├── loader.py               # load_esco() 
    ├── builder.py              # build_graph()
    ├── visualizer.py           # pyvis or JSON export
    └── runner.py               # orchestrates it all

✅ Best of Both Worlds?

Start in a Notebook, and once your logic is clean, extract to .py files.

You said:
(orientor) (base) philippebeliveau@philippes-MacBook-Pro Esco % python runner.py Traceback (most recent call last): File "/Users/philippebeliveau/Desktop/Notebook/Orientor_project/Orientor_project/data_n_notebook/Esco/runner.py", line 4, in <module> from builder import build_graph ImportError: cannot import name 'build_graph' from 'builder' (/Users/philippebeliveau/Desktop/Notebook/Orientor_project/Orientor_project/data_n_notebook/Esco/builder.py)
ChatGPT said:

This error is likely due to how Python is resolving module imports.

You're running runner.py as a top-level script, but it's importing sibling files like builder.py without knowing they’re part of a module.

✅ Fix 1: Use Absolute Imports with a Module Folder

Let’s make sure your project is treated as a Python package.

Step 1: Add an __init__.py file

In your folder Esco/ (where runner.py, builder.py, etc. live), add an empty __init__.py file:

csharp
📁 Esco/
├── __init__.py   ← add this!
├── runner.py
├── builder.py
├── loader.py
├── visualizer.py

This makes it a proper Python package.

Step 2: Run as a module from the parent folder

Navigate one level above Esco and run:

bash
python -m Esco.runner

This tells Python to treat Esco as a module and resolves imports correctly.

✅ Fix 2 (Alternative): Modify sys.path (Quick Hack)

If you're just testing quickly, add this to the top of runner.py:

python
import sys
from pathlib import Path
sys.path.append(str(Path(__file__).resolve().parent))

This manually forces Python to include the local directory for imports. But long-term, prefer Fix 1.

Would you like me to update runner.py with this quick fix, or do you want to go with the clean module-based fix?

Thanks for the full trace — here’s what’s going wrong and how to fix it cleanly in under 2 minutes.

✅ Real Problem

You're inside this folder:

swift
/Users/philippebeliveau/Desktop/Notebook/Orientor_project/Orientor_project/data_n_notebook/Esco/

Trying to run:

bash
python -m runner

But runner.py is trying to import from builder.py without knowing it’s part of a package. Python can't resolve this unless the folder is treated as a proper module.

✅ Cleanest Solution (Reliable & Scalable)

✅ Step 1: Rename the folder to a valid module name (optional but clean)

Make sure your folder is named esco or esco_graph, not Esco, because capitalized names sometimes confuse the module loader.

bash
mv Esco esco_graph

Now your structure should be:

bash
📁 .../Orientor_project/Orientor_project/data_n_notebook/esco_graph/
├── __init__.py           # ← create this file if it doesn't exist
├── runner.py
├── builder.py
├── loader.py
├── visualizer.py

✅ Step 2: Move up one level and run as a module:

bash
cd /Users/philippebeliveau/Desktop/Notebook/Orientor_project/Orientor_project/data_n_notebook
python -m esco_graph.runner

Edit the top of runner.py and patch sys.path:

import sys
from pathlib import Path
sys.path.append(str(Path(__file__).resolve().parent))

from builder import build_graph
from loader import load_esco
from visualizer import visualize_pyvis, export_to_json

Then run it with:

bash
python runner.py

This is fine during prototyping, but not ideal for long-term structure.

Thanks for persisting — we’re very close. Based on the error:

ImportError: cannot import name 'build_graph' from 'builder'

This tells us the file builder.py exists but doesn’t contain a function named build_graph — or Python can’t see it because of a stale or broken file.

✅ Let’s Fix This Step-by-Step

✅ Step 1: Confirm the contents of builder.py

Please open builder.py and verify that it actually contains the following:

python
def build_graph(esco_data):
    ...

If it only has comments, or there’s a typo, Python will throw the import error you’re seeing.

If you want, I can regenerate and show you the full correct contents for builder.py.

✅ Step 2: Ensure __init__.py exists

Make sure you have this in the esco/ folder:

bash
touch __init__.py

This is necessary for Python to treat esco as a package during python -m esco.runner.

✅ Step 3: Use absolute imports inside runner.py

Make sure runner.py has:

python
from esco.builder import build_graph
from esco.loader import load_esco
from esco.visualizer import visualize_pyvis, export_to_json

This only works when you're running:

bash
cd /Users/philippebeliveau/Desktop/Notebook/Orientor_project/Orientor_project/data_n_notebook
python -m esco.runner

from loader import load_esco

ChatGPT said:

Thank you for the full context. You’ve run into a circular or broken import path, and we’re going to cleanly fix this right now.

✅ Your Fastest Path to Fix

Let’s solve this using the quickest, production-safe method:

✅ 1. Replace Imports with Local Relative Imports

In runner.py, replace this:

python
from esco.builder import build_graph
from esco.loader import load_esco
from esco.visualizer import visualize_pyvis, export_to_json

with this:

python
from builder import build_graph
from loader import load_esco
from visualizer import visualize_pyvis, export_to_json

💡 These are local relative imports — and they work when you do:

bash
cd esco
python runner.py

✅ 2. Confirm builder.py Has the Function

Open builder.py and ensure this exists:

python
def build_graph(esco_data):
    import networkx as nx
    G = nx.DiGraph()
    # ... etc.
    return G

If it doesn’t contain a def build_graph(...), Python will throw the exact error you saw:

ImportError: cannot import name 'build_graph'

Let me know if you'd like the full fixed contents re-pasted.

✅ 3. Final Run (from the correct place)

From this folder:

swift
/Users/philippebeliveau/Desktop/Notebook/Orientor_project/Orientor_project/data_n_notebook/esco

Run:

bash
python runner.py

✅ Optional: For Later, Use python -m esco.runner

If you want to use the -m pattern later, you’ll need:

  1. __init__.py inside the esco/ folder

  2. Absolute imports again: from esco.builder import ...

  3. Run from the parent folder:

    bash
    cd data_n_notebook
python -m esco.runner

You said:
(orientor) (base) philippebeliveau@philippes-MacBook-Pro esco % python runner.py Traceback (most recent call last): File "/Users/philippebeliveau/Desktop/Notebook/Orientor_project/Orientor_project/data_n_notebook/esco/runner.py", line 20, in <module> run_pipeline() File "/Users/philippebeliveau/Desktop/Notebook/Orientor_project/Orientor_project/data_n_notebook/esco/runner.py", line 14, in run_pipeline esco_data = load_esco(data_dir) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ TypeError: load_esco() takes 0 positional arguments but 1 was given
ChatGPT said:

Perfect — we’re finally into real execution and the error now is small and clear:

TypeError: load_esco() takes 0 positional arguments but 1 was given

✅ Cause

Your runner.py is calling:

python
load_esco(data_dir)

But the load_esco function you have likely looks like this:

python
def load_esco():
    ...

✅ Fix: Update load_esco in loader.py to accept data_dir

Open loader.py and change this:

python
def load_esco():

to:

python
def load_esco(data_dir):

And you're done.

🧪 Final Check

Then run:

bash
cd esco
python runner.py

and your graph should be built and visualized.

(orientor) (base) philippebeliveau@philippes-MacBook-Pro esco % python runner.py Traceback (most recent call last): File "/Users/philippebeliveau/Desktop/Notebook/Orientor_project/Orientor_project/data_n_notebook/esco/runner.py", line 20, in <module> run_pipeline() File "/Users/philippebeliveau/Desktop/Notebook/Orientor_project/Orientor_project/data_n_notebook/esco/runner.py", line 16, in run_pipeline visualize_pyvis(G, "esco_graph.html") File "/Users/philippebeliveau/Desktop/Notebook/Orientor_project/Orientor_project/data_n_notebook/esco/visualizer.py", line 9, in visualize_pyvis net.add_node(node, label=data['label'], title=data['type'], color="skyblue" if data['type'] == 'skill' else "lightgreen") ~~~~^^^^^^^^^ KeyError: 'label' First, Il show you how the data look like Model Info Contains information about the model. The export filename is model_info.csv Columns UUIDHISTORY: A list of UUIDs. NAME: The name of the model. LOCALE: The short code of the model's locale. DESCRIPTION: The description of the model. VERSION: The version of the model. RELEASED: A boolean value that indicates whether the model is released or not. RELEASENOTES: The release notes of the model. CREATEDAT: The date the model was created. UPDATEDAT: The date the model was last updated. Skills Contains the skills of the taxonomy. The export filename is skills.csv Columns ORIGINURI: A URI that points to the location where the skill was originally defined. ID: A unique identifier, used for referencing the skill within the CSV dataset. UUIDHISTORY: A list of UUIDs. SKILLTYPE: The skill type. Possible values: skill/competence,knowledge,language,attitude or empty ( ). REUSELEVEL: The skill reuse level. Possible values: sector-specific,occupation-specific,cross-sector,transversal or empty ( ). PREFERREDLABEL: The preferred label of the skill. ALTLABELS: A list of alternative labels for the skill. Maximum length per label: 256 characters. Maximum number of labels: 100. DESCRIPTION: The skill description. Maximum length:4000 characters. DEFINITION: The skill definition. Maximum length:4000 characters. SCOPENOTE: The skill scope note. Maximum length:4000 characters. CREATEDAT: The date the skill was created. UPDATEDAT: The date the skill was last updated. Skill Groups Contains the skill groups of the taxonomy. The export filename is skill_groups.csv Columns ORIGINURI: A URI that points to the location where the skill group was originally defined. ID: A unique identifier, used for referencing the skill group within the CSV dataset. UUIDHISTORY: A list of UUIDs. CODE: SkillGroup code as defined in ESCO. It has the general format SX.X.X, where X is a number. PREFERREDLABEL: The preferred label of the skill group. ALTLABELS: A list of alternative labels for the skill group. Maximum length per label: 256 characters. Maximum number of labels: 100. DESCRIPTION: The skill group description. Maximum length:4000 characters. SCOPENOTE: The skill group scope note. Maximum length:4000 characters. CREATEDAT: The date the skill group was created. UPDATEDAT: The date the skill group was last updated. Occupations Contains the occupations of the taxonomy. The export filename is occupations.csv Columns ORIGINURI: A URI that points to the location where the occupation was originally defined. ID: A unique identifier, used for referencing the occupation within the CSV dataset. UUIDHISTORY: A list of UUIDs. ISCOGROUPCODE: A four digit identification code of the ISCO group that the occupation belongs to. CODE: An occupation code assigned to the occupation. PREFERREDLABEL: The preferred label of the occupation. ALTLABELS: A list of alternative labels for the occupation. Maximum length per label: 256 characters. Maximum number of labels: 100. DESCRIPTION: The occupation description. Maximum length:4000 characters. DEFINITION: The occupation definition. Maximum length:4000 characters. SCOPENOTE: The occupation scope note. Maximum length:4000 characters. REGULATEDPROFESSIONNOTE: The regulated profession note. Maximum length:4000 characters. OCCUPATIONTYPE: The type of the occupation. Possible values: escooccupation or localoccupation. ISLOCALIZED: A boolean value that indicates whether the occupation is localized or not. Only ocuppations of the type escooccupation can be localized. Possible values: true or false. CREATEDAT: The date the occupation was created. UPDATEDAT: The date the occupation was last updated. ISCO Groups Contains the ISCO groups of the taxonomy. The export filename is isco_groups.csv Columns ORIGINURI: A URI that points to the location where the ISCO group was originally defined. ID: A unique identifier, used for referencing the ISCO group within the CSV dataset. UUIDHISTORY: A list of UUIDs. CODE: A four digit identification code of the ISCO group. PREFERREDLABEL: The preferred label of the ISCO group. ALTLABELS: A list of alternative labels for the ISCO group. Maximum length per label: 256 characters. Maximum number of labels: 100. DESCRIPTION: The ISCO group description. Maximum length:4000 characters. CREATEDAT: The date the ISCO group was created. UPDATEDAT: The date the ISCO group was last updated. Skill-to-Skill Relations Contains the relations between skills. The export filename is skill_to_skill_relations.csv Columns REQUIRINGID: The ID of the skill that requires another skill. RELATIONTYPE: The type of the relation. Possible values: essential or optional. REQUIREDID: The ID of the skill that is required by another skill. CREATEDAT: The date the relation was created. UPDATEDAT: The date the relation was last updated. Occupation-to-Skill Relations Contains the relations between occupations and skills. The export filename is occupation_to_skill_relations.csv Columns OCCUPATIONTYPE: The type of the occupation. Possible values: escooccupation or localoccupation. OCCUPATIONID: The ID of the occupation. RELATIONTYPE: The type of the relation. Possible values: essential or optional. SKILLID: The ID of the skill. CREATEDAT: The date the relation was created. UPDATEDAT: The date the relation was last updated. Skill Hierarchy Contains the hierarchical structure of various skills. The export filename is skill_hierarchy.csv Columns PARENTOBJECTTYPE: The type of the parent object. Possible values: skill or skillgroup. PARENTID: The ID of the parent object. CHILDID: The ID of the child object. CHILDOBJECTTYPE: The type of the child object. Possible values: skill or skillgroup. CREATEDAT: The date the relation was created. UPDATEDAT: The date the relation was last updated. Caveat: A skill cannot be the parent of a skill group. Occupation Hierarchy Contains the hierarchical structure of various occupations. The export filename is occupation_hierarchy.csv Columns PARENTOBJECTTYPE: The type of the parent object. Possible values: iscogroup, escooccupation, localoccupation. PARENTID: The ID of the parent object. CHILDID: The ID of the child object. CHILDOBJECTTYPE: The type of the child object. Possible values: iscogroup, escooccupation, localoccupation. CREATEDAT: The date the relation was created. UPDATEDAT: The date the relation was last updated.
ChatGPT said:

Excellent — that error is very clear now and easy to fix.

✅ The Root Cause

In your builder.py, we added graph nodes like this:

python
G.add_node(f"skill::{row['ID']}", label=row['PREFERREDLABEL'], type='skill')

But during visualization in visualizer.py, we do:

python
label = data['label']

The error you're getting:

KeyError: 'label'

Means some nodes were likely added without a label attribute.

✅ Why? Because...

In your builder.py, we handled:

  • skills ✅ → used PREFERREDLABEL for label

  • skill_groups ✅

  • occupations ✅

But if any of the nodes (e.g., from hierarchy or relations) were added via an edge like this:

python
G.add_edge("skill::X", "skill::Y", type="requires")

…and those nodes were not already defined, they’ll get added automatically by NetworkX with no attributes — hence no label.

✅ Fix

Update your builder.py with this safety check before every edge add:

python
# Add node safely if it doesn’t exist
def ensure_node(G, node_id, node_type):
    if node_id not in G:
        G.add_node(node_id, label=node_id, type=node_type)

Then before any G.add_edge(...), call:

python
ensure_node(G, parent_id, parent_type)
ensure_node(G, child_id, child_type)
G.add_edge(parent_id, child_id, type='is_parent_of')

Apply this in both hierarchy sections:

  • skill_hierarchy

  • occupation_hierarchy

And in:

  • occupation_to_skill_relations

  • skill_to_skill_relations

✅ Temporary Patch (Quickest)

If you just want to make it work without modifying builder.py right now, you can patch visualizer.py like this:

python
for node, data in G.nodes(data=True):
    label = data.get('label', node)
    node_type = data.get('type', 'unknown')
    color = "skyblue" if node_type == 'skill' else "lightgreen"
    net.add_node(node, label=label, title=node_type, color=color)

That fallback will show the node ID if label is missing.

✅ Your Call

Would you like me to:

  • Update your builder.py to include ensure_node() logic?

  • Or update visualizer.py to be resilient to missing labels?

Let’s patch it properly.

You said:
(orientor) (base) philippebeliveau@philippes-MacBook-Pro esco % python runner.py Traceback (most recent call last): File "/Users/philippebeliveau/miniforge3/envs/orientor/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pandas/core/indexes/base.py", line 3805, in get_loc return self._engine.get_loc(casted_key) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "index.pyx", line 167, in pandas._libs.index.IndexEngine.get_loc File "index.pyx", line 196, in pandas._libs.index.IndexEngine.get_loc File "pandas/_libs/hashtable_class_helper.pxi", line 7081, in pandas._libs.hashtable.PyObjectHashTable.get_item File "pandas/_libs/hashtable_class_helper.pxi", line 7089, in pandas._libs.hashtable.PyObjectHashTable.get_item KeyError: 'PARENTID' The above exception was the direct cause of the following exception: Traceback (most recent call last): File "/Users/philippebeliveau/Desktop/Notebook/Orientor_project/Orientor_project/data_n_notebook/esco/runner.py", line 20, in <module> run_pipeline() File "/Users/philippebeliveau/Desktop/Notebook/Orientor_project/Orientor_project/data_n_notebook/esco/runner.py", line 15, in run_pipeline G = build_graph(esco_data) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/Users/philippebeliveau/Desktop/Notebook/Orientor_project/Orientor_project/data_n_notebook/esco/builder.py", line 24, in build_graph ensure_node(G, row['PARENTID'], row['PARENTOBJECTTYPE']) ~~~^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/Users/philippebeliveau/miniforge3/envs/orientor/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pandas/core/series.py", line 1121, in __getitem__ return self._get_value(key) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/Users/philippebeliveau/miniforge3/envs/orientor/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pandas/core/series.py", line 1237, in _get_value loc = self.index.get_loc(label) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/Users/philippebeliveau/miniforge3/envs/orientor/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pandas/core/indexes/base.py", line 3812, in get_loc raise KeyError(key) from err KeyError: 'PARENTID'
ChatGPT said:

This KeyError: 'PARENTID' clearly tells us that the column 'PARENTID' does not exist in your occupation_hierarchy.csv or skill_hierarchy.csv.

✅ The Real Fix

Pandas column names are case-sensitive, and ESCO sometimes uses capital letters or different naming conventions than expected.

Let’s confirm the actual column names in builder.py before using them.

✅ Step 1: Print the columns before iterating

Modify this line in your builder.py before the loop:

python
print("Skill hierarchy columns:", esco_data['skill_hierarchy'].columns)
print("Occupation hierarchy columns:", esco_data['occupation_hierarchy'].columns)

Then run:

bash
python runner.py

This will print the real column names like:

css
Index(['ParentId', 'ParentObjectType', 'ChildId', 'ChildObjectType', ...])

✅ Step 2: Rename to match

Once you see the actual names, update your loop accordingly. Likely you need:

python
for _, row in esco_data['skill_hierarchy'].iterrows():
    parent_id = row['PARENTID'] if 'PARENTID' in row else row['ParentId']
    child_id = row['CHILDID'] if 'CHILDID' in row else row['ChildId']
    parent_type = row['PARENTOBJECTTYPE'] if 'PARENTOBJECTTYPE' in row else row['ParentObjectType']
    child_type = row['CHILDOBJECTTYPE'] if 'CHILDOBJECTTYPE' in row else row['ChildObjectType']
    
    ensure_node(G, f\"{parent_type}::{parent_id}\", parent_type)
    ensure_node(G, f\"{child_type}::{child_id}\", child_type)
    G.add_edge(f\"{parent_type}::{parent_id}\", f\"{child_type}::{child_id}\", type='is_parent_of')

If you want, I can regenerate the full fixed version of builder.py for you.

Would you like me to do that right now?

You said:
skills.csv ORIGINURI,ID,UUIDHISTORY,SKILLTYPE,REUSELEVEL,PREFERREDLABEL,ALTLABELS,DESCRIPTION,DEFINITION,SCOPENOTE http://data.europa.eu/esco/skill/0005c151-5b5a-4a66-8aac-60e734beb1ab,key_1260,dc2349de-68d3-4503-b132-faaf49e2d1c4,skill/competence,sector-specific,manage musical staff,"manage staff of music coordinate duties of musical staff manage music staff direct musical staff manage musical staff","Assign and manage staff tasks in areas such as scoring, arranging, copying music and vocal coaching.",, skills_hierarchy.csv PARENTOBJECTTYPE,PARENTID,CHILDID,CHILDOBJECTTYPE skillgroup,key_1157,key_620,skillgroup skillgroup,key_620,key_621,skillgroup skillgroup,key_621,key_622,skillgroup skillgroup,key_620,key_623,skillgroup skillgroup,key_623,key_624,skillgroup skillgroup,key_620,key_625,skillgroup skillsGroups.csv ORIGINURI,ID,UUIDHISTORY,CODE,PREFERREDLABEL,ALTLABELS,DESCRIPTION,SCOPENOTE http://data.europa.eu/esco/isced-f/00,key_620,d4d4e095-b603-4be6-b3b8-4f1330a3f19e,,generic programmes and qualifications,,"Generic programmes and qualifications are those providing fundamental and personal skills education which cover a broad range of subjects and do not emphasise or specialise in a particular broad or narrow field. This broad field should not be used as a residual category. Programmes and qualifications with a specific subject emphasis should be classified in broad fields 01 to 10. In particular, education at the tertiary level should only by way of exception be classified here.", http://data.europa.eu/esco/isced-f/000,key_621,ed5520dc-1721-49e4-a8de-fbae36ba7e5e,,generic programmes and qualifications not further defined,,, http://data.europa.eu/esco/isced-f/0000,key_622,d679e2f9-ebc4-4c2b-800f-1732ea94c74b,,generic programmes and qualifications not further defined,,, http://data.europa.eu/esco/isced-f/001,key_623,705aa849-cf24-4219-84c6-3f7a90a8c58c,,basic programmes and qualifications,,, http://data.europa.eu/esco/isced-f/0011,key_624,b4b07d7c-6ef2-4aae-ae12-f52636da59af,,basic programmes and qualifications,,"Basic programmes and qualifications are designed to provide participants with fundamental skills in reading, writing and arithmetic along with an elementary understanding of other subjects such as history, geography, natural science, social science, art and music, and in some cases religious instruction. These programmes and qualifications are normally offered at primary and lower secondary levels. Broad, non-specialised programmes at upper secondary level are also classified here even if there is some concentration on, for example, humanities, social science, natural science etc. Vocational programmes and qualifications are included here only by way of exception. Programmes and qualifications with the following main subject content are classified here: Basic programmes and qualifications Broad, generic (non-specialised) programmes and qualifications General programmes and qualifications with no specific subject emphasis Programmes and qualifications at primary level === Inclusions Basic programmes and qualifications at upper secondary level (in some countries at lower secondary level) with some emphasis on e.g. humanities, social sciences or natural sciences but still covering other fields of knowledge are included in this detailed field. In some countries such programmes or qualifications are offered on a modular basis and should also be classified here. === Exclusions Programmes that are considered as general in the programme orientation sense (non-vocational) but have a clear emphasis on a detailed, narrow or broad field are excluded from this detailed field and included in one of the broad fields 01-10, depending on the subject covered.", http://data.europa.eu/esco/isced-f/002,key_625,181996d3-f1b8-4c64-81f5-d4029ec35594,,literacy and numeracy,,, http://data.europa.eu/esco/isced-f/0021,key_626,e9d59046-6dc8-4e8e-ba10-46c81807d262,,literacy and numeracy,,"Literacy and numeracy are programmes or qualifications arranged mainly for adults, designed to teach fundamental skills in reading, writing and arithmetic. The typical age range of participants can be used to distinguish between detailed field 0011 'Basic programmes and qualifications' and this detailed field. Programmes and qualifications with the following main content are classified here: Basic remedial programmes for youth or adults Literacy Numeracy", http://data.europa.eu/esco/isced-f/003,key_627,386fe5b7-05dc-4bc7-9cc6-f325db7747fd,,personal skills and development,,, http://data.europa.eu/esco/isced-f/0031,key_628,3643ac30-9091-4606-9623-73c024ed3c8c,,personal skills and development,,"Personal skills are defined by reference to the effects on the individual's capacity (mental, social etc.). This detailed field covers personal skills programmes not included in 0011 'Basic programmes and qualifications' or 0021 'Literacy and numeracy', giving key competencies and transferable skills. Programmes and qualifications with the following main content are classified here: Argumentation and presentation Assertiveness training Communication skills Co-operation Development of behavioural capacities Development of mental skills Job-seeking programmes Parenting courses Public speaking Self-esteem skills Social competence Time management === Inclusions Education and training in leadership in the context of personal development is included here. Education and training related to the work place or to work assignments is included in this detailed field if it has more to do with personal development than work development. Personal skills courses can be taught at the workplace, but still be classified here based on their content. Programmes for people with special needs on how to cope with their daily life are included here === Exclusions Study of leadership in the skill_skill_relations.csv REQUIRINGID,RELATIONTYPE,REQUIREDID key_1261,optional,key_12782 key_1265,optional,key_11200 key_1270,optional,key_6049 key_1271,optional,key_2978 occupations.csv ORIGINURI,ID,UUIDHISTORY,ISCOGROUPCODE,CODE,PREFERREDLABEL,ALTLABELS,DESCRIPTION,DEFINITION,SCOPENOTE,REGULATEDPROFESSIONNOTE,OCCUPATIONTYPE,ISLOCALIZED http://data.europa.eu/esco/occupation/00030d09-2b3a-4efd-87cc-c4ea39d27c34,key_15156,583cf200-fe20-4ec5-b5c9-d1a4c4fa4adb,2654,2654.1.7,technical director,"technical and operations director head of technical director of technical arts head of technical department technical supervisor technical director technical manager","Technical directors realise the artistic visions of the creators within technical constraints. They coordinate the operations of various production units, such as scene, wardrobe, sound and lighting, and make-up. They adapt the prototype and study the feasibility, implementation, operation and technical monitoring of the artistic project. They are also responsible for the stage equipment and technical equipment.",,,http://data.europa.eu/esco/regulated-professions/unregulated,escooccupation,false http://data.europa.eu/esco/occupation/000e93a3-d956-4e45-aacb-f12c83fedf84,key_15157,e2db15f0-9c6f-498f-b44c-cd46f1c88b51,8121,8121.4,metal drawing machine operator,"metal drawing machine operator metal drawing machine technician occupations_hierarchy.csv PARENTOBJECTTYPE,PARENTID,CHILDID,CHILDOBJECTTYPE iscogroup,key_1,key_2,iscogroup iscogroup,key_2,key_3,iscogroup occupations_skills_relations.csv OCCUPATIONTYPE,OCCUPATIONID,RELATIONTYPE,SKILLID escooccupation,key_15156,essential,key_15086 escooccupation,key_15156,essential,key_1525 escooccupation,key_15156,essential,key_3388 isco_groups.csv ORIGINURI,ID,UUIDHISTORY,CODE,PREFERREDLABEL,ALTLABELS,DESCRIPTION http://data.europa.eu/esco/isco/C0,key_1,375ad4ae-0113-4d76-b42a-e7b9ad8178bd,0,Armed forces occupations,,"Armed forces occupations include all jobs held by members of the armed forces. Members of the armed forces are those personnel who are currently serving in the armed forces, including auxiliary services, whether on a voluntary or compulsory basis, and who are not free to accept civilian employment and are subject to military discipline. Included are regular members of the army, navy, air force and other military services, as well as conscripts enrolled for military training or other service for a specified period. Occupations in this major group are classified into the following sub-major groups: 01 Commissioned Armed Forces Officers 02 Non-commissioned Armed Forces Officers 03 Armed Forces Occupations, Other Ranks Excluded from this group are: - jobs held by persons in civilian employment of government establishments concerned with defence issues; - police (other than military police); - customs inspectors and members of border or other armed civilian services. Notes Many jobs performed by members of the armed forces are similar, in terms of the nature of the work performed, to civilian occupations such as medical doctors, radio operators, cooks, secretaries and heavy truck drivers. Conceptually, it may be appropriate, therefore, to classify such jobs in the armed forces with similar civilian jobs. This approach is adopted in several national occupation classifications. Typically such classifications also identify a number of military-specific occupational groups. In many countries, however, it is not possible to produce information about the nature of the work performed by members of the armed forces. In adapting ISCO-08 for national purposes countries may wish, therefore, to consider what approach best suits their circumstances and user needs. For purposes of international comparability, however, where data pertaining to members of the armed forces are reported and classified by occupation, they should be included, where possible, in ISCO-08 Major Group 0: Armed Forces Occupations.   • Uses the correct filenames (skills_hierarchy.csv, occupations_skills_relations.csv, etc.).

  • Avoids the 'PARENTID' KeyError by using the proper column names from your files.

  • Handles missing nodes gracefully with ensure_node().

python runner.py

[LOG] Loading ESCO CSV files from: /Users/philippebeliveau/Desktop/Notebook/Orientor_project/Esco-Oasis/taxonomy-model-application/data-sets/csv/esco-v1.1.2 [LOG] Building graph from ESCO data... [LOG] Adding skills... [LOG] Generating interactive graph visualization: esco_graph.html esco_graph.html Traceback (most recent call last): File "/Users/philippebeliveau/Desktop/Notebook/Orientor_project/Orientor_project/data_n_notebook/esco/runner.py", line 25, in <module> run_pipeline() File "/Users/philippebeliveau/Desktop/Notebook/Orientor_project/Orientor_project/data_n_notebook/esco/runner.py", line 19, in run_pipeline visualize_pyvis(G, "esco_graph.html") File "/Users/philippebeliveau/Desktop/Notebook/Orientor_project/Orientor_project/data_n_notebook/esco/visualizer.py", line 20, in visualize_pyvis net.show(output_html) File "/Users/philippebeliveau/miniforge3/envs/orientor/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pyvis/network.py", line 546, in show self.write_html(name, open_browser=False,notebook=True) File "/Users/philippebeliveau/miniforge3/envs/orientor/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pyvis/network.py", line 515, in write_html self.html = self.generate_html(notebook=notebook) ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ File "/Users/philippebeliveau/miniforge3/envs/orientor/lib/python3.11/site-packages/pyvis/network.py", line 479, in generate_html self.html = template.render(height=height, ^^^^^^^^^^^^^^^ AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'render'
ChatGPT said:

You're getting this error because PyVis can't find the Jinja2 template engine it depends on:

AttributeError: 'NoneType' object has no attribute 'render'

This usually happens when:

  • Jinja2 is not installed,

  • the HTML template that PyVis uses is missing or broken.

✅ Fix It

Step 1: Install jinja2 explicitly

Run:

bash
pip install jinja2

This will ensure PyVis can load its HTML rendering templates properly.

Step 2: Confirm PyVis is fully functional

If jinja2 was already installed, try reinstalling PyVis and Jinja2 together:

bash
pip uninstall pyvis jinja2 -y
pip install pyvis jinja2

Step 3: Optional — Switch to file-only export (headless)

If you're still running into issues and don't need to open the browser automatically, replace:

python
net.show(output_html)

with:

python
net.write_html(output_html)

This prevents the rendering engine from trying to open a browser or use notebooks.

Would you like me to make this change (write-only mode) directly into visualizer.py in your pipeline?

You said:
how to visualize it now (orientor) (base) philippebeliveau@philippes-MacBook-Pro esco % python runner.py [LOG] Starting ESCO pipeline... [LOG] Loading ESCO CSV files from: /Users/philippebeliveau/Desktop/Notebook/Orientor_project/Esco-Oasis/taxonomy-model-application/data-sets/csv/esco-v1.1.2 [LOG] Building graph from ESCO data... [LOG] Adding skills... [LOG] Generating interactive graph visualization: esco_graph.html [LOG] Visualization complete. [LOG] Exporting graph to JSON file: esco_graph.json [LOG] Export complete. Pipeline executed in 35.90 seconds.
ChatGPT said:

Perfect — your pipeline ran successfully and generated:

  • esco_graph.html → an interactive visualization

  • esco_graph.json → a structured export of the graph

✅ How to Visualize It

🔍 Option 1: Open the HTML directly in your browser

From your terminal, run:

bash
open esco_graph.html

If you're on macOS, this will open the interactive graph in your default browser. On Windows, use:

bash
start esco_graph.html

Or just double-click the file in Finder.

🔁 Option 2: Serve it on a local server (good for testing frontends)

If you want to host it temporarily, from your esco/ folder run:

bash
python -m http.server 8000

Then go to:

bash
http://localhost:8000/esco_graph.html

👁 Option 3: Load the esco_graph.json into D3.js or Cytoscape

This is ideal if you're planning to embed the graph in a custom frontend or add filters.

Would you like help next with:

  • Filtering what gets visualized (e.g., a specific occupation group)?

  • Using esco_graph.json in a React or D3-based frontend?

You said:
It seems stuck at 0%
ChatGPT said:

Thanks for the screenshot — this “0% stuck loading bar” issue in PyVis is known, especially when the graph is large or contains malformed entries (like NaN, empty strings, or very long strings).

✅ Likely Causes

  1. Too Many Nodes → The graph has thousands of nodes (ESCO has ~14k skills).

  2. Missing or non-string labels → PyVis tries to render nodes with None, NaN, or float types as labels.

  3. Circular/NaN data in edges or properties → Can break rendering silently.

✅ Fix in 3 Quick Steps

✅ Step 1: Sanitize Labels in builder.py

Modify where nodes are added to force labels to be strings and fill missing ones:

python
def clean_label(value):
    return str(value).strip() if pd.notna(value) and value != "" else "unknown"

# Example for skills
label = clean_label(row['PREFERREDLABEL'])
G.add_node(f"skill::{row['ID']}", label=label, type='skill')

Do this for skills, skill_groups, and occupations.

✅ Step 2: Filter Graph for Debugging

Before rendering everything, just visualize a subset to test:

python
def filter_graph(G, max_nodes=200):
    H = nx.DiGraph()
    for i, node in enumerate(G.nodes(data=True)):
        if i >= max_nodes:
            break
        H.add_node(node[0], **node[1])
    for u, v, d in G.edges(data=True):
        if u in H and v in H:
            H.add_edge(u, v, **d)
    return H

Then in runner.py, replace:

python
G = build_graph(esco_data)

with:

python
G_full = build_graph(esco_data)
G = filter_graph(G_full, max_nodes=200)

This will help you test rendering without crashing the browser.

✅ Step 3: Use net.write_html() instead of net.show()

In visualizer.py, replace:

python
net.show(output_html)

with:

python
net.write_html(output_html)

This disables automatic notebook or browser interaction and creates a leaner file.

Would you like me to apply all 3 fixes into your pipeline directly?

You said:
it says (orientor) (base) philippebeliveau@philippes-MacBook-Pro esco % python -m http.server 8000 Serving HTTP on :: port 8000 (http://[::]:8000/) ... ::1 - - [01/May/2025 10:24:28] "GET / HTTP/1.1" 200 - ::1 - - [01/May/2025 10:24:30] "GET /esco_graph.html HTTP/1.1" 304 - ::1 - - [01/May/2025 10:24:30] "GET /lib/bindings/utils.js HTTP/1.1" 304 -
ChatGPT said:

This confirms the root issue: your esco_graph.html references external JS files (like /lib/bindings/utils.js) that don’t exist locally, causing the loading bar to get stuck at 0%.

This is a known PyVis behavior. The library references vis.js dependencies via a lib/ path that's only available when running inside a Jupyter notebook — but you're serving it via http.server, so it breaks.

✅ Clean Fix: Inline the JS to Make the HTML Self-Contained

Tell PyVis to embed everything inline:

🔧 Modify visualizer.py

Replace:

python
net = Network(notebook=False, directed=True)

with:

python
net = Network(notebook=False, directed=True, cdn_resources='in_line')

This embeds the vis.js and utility code directly into the HTML file instead of referencing missing /lib/ paths.

Then re-run:

bash
python runner.py

And refresh:

bash
http://localhost:8000/esco_graph.html

✅ Optional: Reduce node count during test

To prevent lag, still keep the filter_graph() idea with max_nodes=200 until you confirm rendering works.

Would you like me to apply both the inline JS fix and filtering for you now in the canvas code?

ChatGPT said:
Esco Graph Pipeline
